# [clang] Pack indexing expressions are not mangled

**Author:** yijan4845
**URL:** https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/issues/112003

## Body

**This testcase is generated by a fuzzer.**

Compiler Explorer: [https://godbolt.org/z/EG7rrdfhb](https://godbolt.org/z/EG7rrdfhb)

This valid code for C++2c will crash on Clang Assertion Trunk:
```cpp
template <int I, auto...V>
decltype(V...[I]) foo() {}

void fn() {
    foo<0, 0>();
}
```

Stack dump:
```
unexpected statement kind
UNREACHABLE executed at /root/llvm-project/clang/lib/AST/ItaniumMangle.cpp:4785!
PLEASE submit a bug report to https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/issues/ and include the crash backtrace, preprocessed source, and associated run script.
Stack dump:
0.	Program arguments: /opt/compiler-explorer/clang-assertions-trunk/bin/clang++ -gdwarf-4 -g -o /app/output.s -mllvm --x86-asm-syntax=intel -fno-verbose-asm -S --gcc-toolchain=/opt/compiler-explorer/gcc-snapshot -fcolor-diagnostics -fno-crash-diagnostics -O3 <source>
1.	<eof> parser at end of file
2.	<source>:4:6: LLVM IR generation of declaration 'fn'
3.	<source>:4:6: Generating code for declaration 'fn'
4.	<source>:2:19: Mangling declaration 'foo'

@llvm/issue-subscribers-clang-frontend

Author: Yihan Yang (yijan4845)

<details>
**This testcase is generated by a fuzzer.**

This valid code for C++2c will crash on Clang Assertion Trunk:
```cpp
template &lt;int I, auto...V&gt;
decltype(V...[I]) foo() {}

void fn() {
    foo&lt;0, 0&gt;();
}
```

---

### Comment 2 - zyn0217

@cor3ntin We don't have rules for mangling pack indexing expressions, do we?

---

### Comment 3 - zyn0217

Aha, find it: https://github.com/itanium-cxx-abi/cxx-abi/issues/175

---

### Comment 4 - cor3ntin

@AaronBallman @erichkeane @zygoloid no reply from the itanium group. Should we come up with something ourselves and/or use a vendor extension?

---

### Comment 5 - erichkeane

@rjmccall is our Itanium ABI owner, can he respond here?

I THINK what I'd prefer is that we come up with something ourselves, submit it as a patch to the Itanium ABI, and implement that.  

---

### Comment 6 - rjmccall

The "Itanium group" is not a roomful of dedicated engineers twiddling their thumbs and waiting to invent manglings for whoever walks by.  It's a communications channel for the engineers working on features — and congratulations, this now means you — to collaborate and try to maintain interoperation.  So yes, what you should do is send a concrete proposal to the group, and we'll give feedback and try to identify problems, and if there aren't any problems we'll just accept it.  Make sure you check out (and include in your proposal!) what other compilers are emitting for this construct, which fortunately is very easy now with godbolt.

---

### Comment 7 - AaronBallman

> The "Itanium group" is not a roomful of dedicated engineers twiddling their thumbs and waiting to invent manglings for whoever walks by. It's a communications channel for the engineers working on features — and congratulations, this now means you — to collaborate and try to maintain interoperation. So yes, what you should do is send a concrete proposal to the group, and we'll give feedback and try to identify problems, and if there aren't any problems we'll just accept it. Make sure you check out (and include in your proposal!) what other compilers are emitting for this construct, which fortunately is very easy now with godbolt.

I think what Erich and Corentin are asking is if you could help determine what a reasonable starting point is so one of them could make a quality proposal to the Itanium group. It's not really documented anywhere in the Itanium group that people are expected to come prepared with a solution, and not everyone feels qualified to make such a proposal, and as the Clang community's Itanium ABI maintainer, you're in a good position to help out.

---

### Comment 8 - rjmccall

Sure, I help walk you through this.  But I want to reiterate that this is part of the job of implementing C++ language features.  I got involved in the Itanium group because I was implementing C++ support in Clang and needed to figure out how I was supposed to mangle different things that I could see in the grammar, so I posted questions to the (then) mailing list, realized that some of those cases just weren't covered, and made suggestions about how they should be mangled.  It's not significantly different from opening a PR to Clang.  In this case, since there's already an issue on GitHub, you should just make your proposal as a comment on that issue; otherwise, you would start by making a new issue.  When the group has decided that we like your proposal, we'll ask you to open a PR with the exact changes you want to make.

The way to start as an implementor is to enumerate the cases that you can see in the AST.  What new productions has this feature added that aren't covered by the Itanium ABI today?  In this case, I can say that it looks like there's at least one new kind of expression and at least one new kind of type, but you as an implementor of the feature are going to have a much better idea of that then I will.  If we're modeling cases differently in the AST — whether that's with different nodes or just a flag on a shared node — it's likely something that we need to consider in the ABI, even if it's just to ultimately decide that the cases can share a mangling.  All of this should go in your proposal post.

For the concrete mangling proposal, the first question is whether this is an official feature or a vendor extension.  In this case, it's an official feature, so it should be directly supported by the grammar.  You then just look at the existing grammar for the kind of thing you're adding and figure out a way to unambiguously represent the new structures.

For example, for the pack index expression, you're adding a new kind of expression, so you want to look at the `<expression>` grammar.  You *could* add a new production to `<expression>`, such as this:

```
<expression> ::= ip <*pack* expression> <*index* expression> # pack...[index]
```

But since this is a new operator, you could instead just add it as a new `<operator-name>`, similar to how `operator[]` is handled as `ix`:

```
<operator-name> ::= ip # ...[] (pack index operator)
```

Adding it as an `<operator-name>` is required if the operator can be used as an overloadable operator.  There isn't really any harm to using it in other cases, though, so if there's even a possibility that it could be used as an overloadable operator in the future, I would definitely do it this way.  They end up mangling the components the same way, though.

In both cases, `ip` is just an arbitrary two-letter sequence I chose that's not currently a valid prefix for anything else in the expression grammar.  `pi` would be the more natural sequence, but it's already used in a context that allows expressions, and while I'm not certain it's possible for them to actually conflict, there's no reason to invite ambiguity.

For types, you'll need a new top-level `<type>` production.  This mostly means you need to pick a prefix that's unambiguous with any other production under `<type>`; you'll see in the grammar that we've generally been adding these as `D<something>`, so just look through the grammar and pick something that's not already picked.

Like I said above, you as an implementor have the best idea of all the cases that we'll need to support in the grammar, so you should look through the code and make an earnest attempt to go through this exercise and add a mangling for everything added by this feature.

When you finally make your PR, it will usually just be a tiny change to the grammar, because most new features don't need extensive explanatory text.  People interested in how we got there will be able to go back to the original issue (which would be good to think in the PR commit message).

In the stalled-out issue, I raised the question of how we handle partial substitutions. I expect that we'll have to have a complicated conversation about that in the group, but it won't really get off the ground until we have a concrete proposal about the feature.

---

### Comment 9 - cor3ntin

Thanks @rjmccall.

I spent some time looking at it, I came up with that

```
- If the pack indexing is not dependent we should mangle the underlying type/expression directly.

Otherwise
<type>  ::= DP <type> <expression>  # pack indexing type
(ie DP<pattern><index>)

For an expression
<expression> ::= pI <expression> <expression> # pack indexing expression
(ie pI<pattern><index>)
```

I reached out to GCC folks to see if that works for them



---

### Comment 10 - rjmccall

I assume the expression production needs to allow an expression as the pattern operand, not a type.

The existing practice in the document is to put clarifying words in italics in the grammar. For example, `*function* name` in the [grammar for `encoding`](http://itanium-cxx-abi.github.io/cxx-abi/abi.html#mangling-structure), or `*virtual offset* number` in the [grammar for `v-offset`](http://itanium-cxx-abi.github.io/cxx-abi/abi.html#mangling-special-thunks).

I assume that a non-dependent pack indexing expression can only happen after substitution of an outer template?  That seems right that it should be folded to the underlying type or expression. This is getting into the partial-substitution domain that we'll probably need to comprehensively specify better, though.

### Comment 12 - zygoloid

It'd be nice if the type and expression forms used manglings that were more similar. I guess you picked `DP` to echo the `Dp` we use for `...type`, but we use `sp` for `...expr` and `sP` is already used by something else. Maybe:

```
<type> ::= Dx <pack type> <index expression>   # pack indexing
<expression> ::= sx <pack expression> <index expression>   # pack indexing
```

I believe both of those are unused, and this harmonizes with all of `Dp` for `...type`, `sp` for `...expr`, and `ix` for `expr[I]`.

### Comment 14 - cor3ntin

So, it occurred to me that we might actually want to model on `sizeof...`.
ie something like

```
<type> ::= Dy <type> <index expression>   # pack indexing
<expression> ::= sy <template-param> <index expression>   # pack indexing
<expression> ::= sy <function-param> <index expression>   # pack indexing
```

Except I don't understand how `sizeof...` works

```cpp
template <int I, auto...T>
decltype(sizeof...(T)) g() {}

template <int I, typename...T>
decltype(sizeof...(T)) h() {}

void fn() {
    g<0, 42, 42>(); // decltype(sizeof...(42, 42...)) g<0, 42, 42>():
    h<0, int, int>(); // decltype(sizeof...(int, int)) h<0, int, int>():
}
```
Notice the ellipsis in `42, 42...` that is not in the type version. 

there is also the issue that using `T1, T2 [N]` as demangled syntax makes no sense, we probably want to render it
as `...(T1, T2)[N]` or something like that

### Comment 16 - AaronBallman

Okay, so this is interesting.

GCC cannot demangle `_Z1gILi0ETpTnDaJLi42ELi42EEEDTsZT0_Ev` but Clang demangles it to `decltype(sizeof...(42, 42...)) g<0, 42, 42>()` where there's an ellipsis after the `42`.

If you change `auto` to `int`, then it mangles to `_Z1gILi0EJLi42ELi42EEEDTsZT0_Ev` which Clang demangles to `decltype(sizeof...(42, 42)) g<0, 42, 42>()` and GCC demangles to `decltype (2) g<0, 42, 42>()`. Note the lack of an ellipsis after the `42` in Clang.

I've not tried to manually mangle or demangle `_Z1gILi0ETpTnDaJLi42ELi42EEEDTsZT0_Ev` yet, but it seems like there's a bug somewhere.

### Comment 17 - cor3ntin

@zygoloid Any idea what's happening here? Thanks!

### Comment 18 - zygoloid

The two manglings are:
```
_Z1gI Li0E TpTnDa J Li42E Li42E E E DT sZ T0_ E v
_Z1gI Li0E        J Li42E Li42E E E DT sZ T0_ E v
```
The first one means: `g<0 with its natural template parameter, {42, 42} with the parameter 'auto...'>`.
The second one means: `g<0 with its natural template parameter, {42, 42} with its natural template parameter>`.
See https://github.com/itanium-cxx-abi/cxx-abi/pull/166 for the rules about natural template parameters.

In either case, we're then mangling the `sizeof...(T)` as `sZ T0_ E`. And it looks like:
- libstdc++'s demangler computes the value of `sizeof...(T)` in this case and uses that in the demangling.
- libc++abi's demangler substitutes in the template arguments as a comma-separated list. It also has [some logic](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/blob/775d0f36f74851172f84074d90cde29e181b3edd/libcxxabi/src/demangle/ItaniumDemangle.h#L1504) to insert a trailing `...` if it doesn't find any packs when doing pack expansion which might be kicking in here. But from a few moments looking at the code it's not obvious to me why it would kick in in one case but not the other.

But regardless, the output of the demangler is best-effort, and I'm not sure there's an especially good way to demangle this given the apparent desire to perform some partial substitution as part of demangling.

---
